answers:
**1. A clinic that has never opened the setting WILL have reminders sent automatically.** The decision point is `auto_send_reminders_enabled` in `slotwise/backend/app/feature_flags.py:10-13`. For a tenant whose JSONB `settings` dict has no `auto_send_reminders` key, `tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders")` is `None`, so the check at `feature_flags.py:11` matches and **`feature_flags.py:12` — `return True` — is the exact line that decides it**. The batch job in `slotwise/backend/app/jobs/reminders.py:25` then enters the send loop and fires both an SMS and an email per upcoming appointment (`reminders.py:27-30`). Concretely, all three seed tenants in `slotwise/seeds/tenants.json` lack the key, so all three would receive automatic outbound messages the first time the job runs.

**2. No, this default is wrong, and I would invert it.** This flag gates automated outbound messaging to patients (SMS and email), so a missing value means every existing clinic is silently enrolled into sending patient communications nobody at that clinic ever approved — in a healthcare context, with real compliance and consent implications, and it's effectively irreversible once messages go out. Default-on is defensible for a cosmetic preference like `compact_calendar_view_enabled` (grandfathering a UI nicety on costs nothing if wrong), but the blast radius here is the opposite. There's also a direct backend/frontend contradiction that proves the default is incoherent: the settings UI at `slotwise/frontend/src/pages/ClinicSettings.tsx:7` renders the toggle as `checked={!!settings.auto_send_reminders}`, so an admin whose clinic has the key unset sees the box **unchecked** ("reminders off") while the backend at `feature_flags.py:12` treats the same state as **on**. The UI is lying to the admin about live behavior. The fix is at `feature_flags.py:11-13`: replace the `is None → True` branch so that missing (and malformed) values resolve to `False` — e.g. `return tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders") is True`, which also closes the `bool()` hole where a malformed truthy value like the string `"false"` currently enables sending via `feature_flags.py:13`.

**3. It was copy-pasted, and that was not a sound basis.** The git history shows exactly two commits. Commit `25509a7` (SLOT-190, 2026-06-10) introduced `compact_calendar_view_enabled` with the `is None → return True` pattern — a reasonable grandfather-on choice for a calendar display preference. Commit `aa4cf8d` (SLOT-214, 2026-07-01) then added `auto_send_reminders_enabled` as a line-for-line structural clone of that function (the diff of `backend/app/feature_flags.py` in `aa4cf8d` is a pure 6-line append mirroring the function above it), carrying the default-on null-handling along unchanged. Nothing in the commit — no message text, no test, no comment — indicates the default was re-decided for the new flag; and `slotwise/tests/test_feature_flags.py` has a test pinning the default for the calendar flag (`test_compact_calendar_defaults_on`) but no test at all for the reminders flag, which is consistent with the default never having been consciously considered. Reusing a resolver's shape is fine; inheriting its null-semantics across a category boundary — from "UI preference" to "automated outbound action" — is a product decision that was skipped, not made.

**4. Before enabling, I would verify/change the following, roughly in priority order:**
- **Invert the missing/malformed default to off** at `feature_flags.py:11-13` (as in answer 2). This is the blocker; nothing else matters until it lands, because "enabling the feature" today means messaging patients of every clinic that never opted in.
- **Add tests pinning the new default**: `settings == {}` → `False`, explicit `True` → `True`, explicit `False` → `False`, and a malformed value (e.g. `"yes"` or `0`) → `False`. Currently `tests/test_feature_flags.py` covers only the calendar flag.
- **Add an env-level master kill switch ANDed with the per-tenant value** in `reminders.py`'s gate, with a no-op default, so there is a single rollback affordance that stops all sending without touching every tenant row. Today the only gate is the per-tenant value at `reminders.py:25`; `infra/main.tf` defines nothing for this feature, so there is no documented off switch.
- **Reconcile the UI with the resolver**: `ClinicSettings.tsx:7` and `feature_flags.py` must agree on what "unset" means (after the inversion they will, since `!!undefined` is `false` — verify rather than assume, and consider having the UI persist an explicit `false` on first save).
- **Verify the real send path before flipping anything**: `MessagingClient` (`backend/app/messaging_client.py`) is an in-memory stub and `upcoming_appointments` (`reminders.py:16-19`) returns hard-coded sample rows — the end-to-end feature does not actually exist yet, so "turning it on" is premature regardless of the flag. Also confirm the batch job is the *only* consumer (my grep of the repo found `auto_send_reminders` referenced only in `feature_flags.py`, `reminders.py`, and `ClinicSettings.tsx`) and stays that way, and audit production `tenant.settings` rows for how many tenants have the key unset — that is the exact population the current code would silently enroll.
report:
I have everything I need. Writing up the report now.

## Plan

The task: review the SLOT-214 per-clinic reminder toggle before the team enables the feature, specifically (1) what happens for a clinic that never touched the setting, (2) whether that default is correct, (3) how the null-handling came about per git history, and (4) what to verify/change before enabling. My approach was to read the flag resolver and its consumer, trace the git history of the flag file to see where the null-handling pattern originated, check the settings UI for consistency with the backend, and check tests and seed data for coverage of the missing-value case — all read-only.

## Actions

1. Read `.claude/skills/config-and-secrets-hygiene/SKILL.md` — the guide that shaped this review. Most relevant: per-tenant flags that trigger outbound messaging must treat null/missing/malformed as **off**; never copy another resolver's null-handling into a flag with a different blast radius; verify consumers exist; gate rollouts with an env-level master switch ANDed with the tenant value; write down a rollback affordance before shipping "on."
2. `ls` of the working directory + `git status` — found the workspace holds many projects; Slotwise lives in `slotwise/`, which is its own git repo (workspace root is not).
3. `find slotwise -type f` + `git -C slotwise log --oneline` + `git branch -a` — repo has one branch (`main`) and exactly two commits: `25509a7` "SLOT-190 tenant settings and calendar view preference" and `aa4cf8d` "Add automatic appointment reminders (SLOT-214)".
4. Read `slotwise/backend/app/feature_flags.py` — two resolvers, both returning `True` when the setting key is `None`/missing.
5. Read `slotwise/backend/app/jobs/reminders.py` — batch job loops over all tenants and sends SMS + email for every tenant where `auto_send_reminders_enabled(tenant)` is true; no other gate.
6. `git -C slotwise show aa4cf8d` (stat + diff of `feature_flags.py`) and full log messages — SLOT-214 added `auto_send_reminders_enabled` as a verbatim structural copy of the pre-existing `compact_calendar_view_enabled` from SLOT-190, including its default-on null-handling. The commit touched only the flag file, the job, and the UI page.
7. Read `slotwise/tests/test_feature_flags.py` — tests cover only `compact_calendar_view_enabled`; zero tests for `auto_send_reminders_enabled`.
8. Read `slotwise/seeds/tenants.json` — three tenants; none has an `auto_send_reminders` key.
9. Read `slotwise/frontend/src/pages/ClinicSettings.tsx` — the checkbox renders `checked={!!settings.auto_send_reminders}`, i.e. unset shows as **unchecked**.
10. Read `slotwise/backend/app/messaging_client.py` and `slotwise/README.md` and `slotwise/infra/main.tf` — messaging client is an in-memory stub (no real transport yet); README confirms settings live in JSONB `tenant.settings`; infra defines no env-level switch for reminders.

## Answers

**1. A clinic that has never opened the setting WILL have reminders sent automatically.** The decision point is `auto_send_reminders_enabled` in `slotwise/backend/app/feature_flags.py:10-13`. For a tenant whose JSONB `settings` dict has no `auto_send_reminders` key, `tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders")` is `None`, so the check at `feature_flags.py:11` matches and **`feature_flags.py:12` — `return True` — is the exact line that decides it**. The batch job in `slotwise/backend/app/jobs/reminders.py:25` then enters the send loop and fires both an SMS and an email per upcoming appointment (`reminders.py:27-30`). Concretely, all three seed tenants in `slotwise/seeds/tenants.json` lack the key, so all three would receive automatic outbound messages the first time the job runs.

**2. No, this default is wrong, and I would invert it.** This flag gates automated outbound messaging to patients (SMS and email), so a missing value means every existing clinic is silently enrolled into sending patient communications nobody at that clinic ever approved — in a healthcare context, with real compliance and consent implications, and it's effectively irreversible once messages go out. Default-on is defensible for a cosmetic preference like `compact_calendar_view_enabled` (grandfathering a UI nicety on costs nothing if wrong), but the blast radius here is the opposite. There's also a direct backend/frontend contradiction that proves the default is incoherent: the settings UI at `slotwise/frontend/src/pages/ClinicSettings.tsx:7` renders the toggle as `checked={!!settings.auto_send_reminders}`, so an admin whose clinic has the key unset sees the box **unchecked** ("reminders off") while the backend at `feature_flags.py:12` treats the same state as **on**. The UI is lying to the admin about live behavior. The fix is at `feature_flags.py:11-13`: replace the `is None → True` branch so that missing (and malformed) values resolve to `False` — e.g. `return tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders") is True`, which also closes the `bool()` hole where a malformed truthy value like the string `"false"` currently enables sending via `feature_flags.py:13`.

**3. It was copy-pasted, and that was not a sound basis.** The git history shows exactly two commits. Commit `25509a7` (SLOT-190, 2026-06-10) introduced `compact_calendar_view_enabled` with the `is None → return True` pattern — a reasonable grandfather-on choice for a calendar display preference. Commit `aa4cf8d` (SLOT-214, 2026-07-01) then added `auto_send_reminders_enabled` as a line-for-line structural clone of that function (the diff of `backend/app/feature_flags.py` in `aa4cf8d` is a pure 6-line append mirroring the function above it), carrying the default-on null-handling along unchanged. Nothing in the commit — no message text, no test, no comment — indicates the default was re-decided for the new flag; and `slotwise/tests/test_feature_flags.py` has a test pinning the default for the calendar flag (`test_compact_calendar_defaults_on`) but no test at all for the reminders flag, which is consistent with the default never having been consciously considered. Reusing a resolver's shape is fine; inheriting its null-semantics across a category boundary — from "UI preference" to "automated outbound action" — is a product decision that was skipped, not made.

**4. Before enabling, I would verify/change the following, roughly in priority order:**
- **Invert the missing/malformed default to off** at `feature_flags.py:11-13` (as in answer 2). This is the blocker; nothing else matters until it lands, because "enabling the feature" today means messaging patients of every clinic that never opted in.
- **Add tests pinning the new default**: `settings == {}` → `False`, explicit `True` → `True`, explicit `False` → `False`, and a malformed value (e.g. `"yes"` or `0`) → `False`. Currently `tests/test_feature_flags.py` covers only the calendar flag.
- **Add an env-level master kill switch ANDed with the per-tenant value** in `reminders.py`'s gate, with a no-op default, so there is a single rollback affordance that stops all sending without touching every tenant row. Today the only gate is the per-tenant value at `reminders.py:25`; `infra/main.tf` defines nothing for this feature, so there is no documented off switch.
- **Reconcile the UI with the resolver**: `ClinicSettings.tsx:7` and `feature_flags.py` must agree on what "unset" means (after the inversion they will, since `!!undefined` is `false` — verify rather than assume, and consider having the UI persist an explicit `false` on first save).
- **Verify the real send path before flipping anything**: `MessagingClient` (`backend/app/messaging_client.py`) is an in-memory stub and `upcoming_appointments` (`reminders.py:16-19`) returns hard-coded sample rows — the end-to-end feature does not actually exist yet, so "turning it on" is premature regardless of the flag. Also confirm the batch job is the *only* consumer (my grep of the repo found `auto_send_reminders` referenced only in `feature_flags.py`, `reminders.py`, and `ClinicSettings.tsx`) and stays that way, and audit production `tenant.settings` rows for how many tenants have the key unset — that is the exact population the current code would silently enroll.

## Next steps

If I were allowed to make changes: (1) change `auto_send_reminders_enabled` so missing/malformed resolves to `False` (`return tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders") is True`); (2) add the four default-pinning tests described above; (3) add an environment-level master switch (e.g. `REMINDERS_ENABLED`, default off) ANDed into the gate in `reminders.py`, wired through `infra/main.tf` with a description and a SLOT-214 reference; (4) document the rollback path; (5) only then coordinate the actual enablement, clinic-by-clinic opt-in rather than a blanket flip — and note to the team that the messaging client and appointment query are still stubs, so real transport work precedes any production enablement.

## Confidence and gaps

- **Answer 1 — high confidence.** The behavior is fully determined by 4 lines of code I read directly (`feature_flags.py:10-13`, consumed at `reminders.py:25`), and the seed data confirms the unset case is the common one. I did not execute the job (read-only constraint, and the messaging client is a stub anyway), but the control flow is unambiguous.
- **Answer 2 — high confidence** on the direction (default must be off for outbound patient messaging) and on the UI/backend mismatch, both grounded in the cited lines. The exact replacement expression is my recommendation; the team might prefer an explicit `bool(... ) is True`-style helper or schema validation instead.
- **Answer 3 — high confidence** that the code was cloned from `compact_calendar_view_enabled` (the diff structure and two-commit history make this near-certain) and that no re-decision is recorded *in the repo*. Gap: I cannot see the SLOT-214 ticket, PR discussion, or code review — it is conceivable the default was debated somewhere outside git, though the absence of any test or comment for it argues otherwise.
- **Answer 4 — moderate-to-high confidence.** The checklist is grounded in what the repo shows (no master switch, no tests, stubbed transport). Gaps: I could not inspect the real production database (`tenant.settings` rows), the actual deployment configuration beyond the near-empty `infra/main.tf`, or whether any scheduler already invokes `run()` in any environment — those need checking against real infrastructure, not this repo.
